title: Write Equations
why: To write an equation, you turn a word story into a number sentence with an equals sign — and that builds on finding a missing number in a number sentence like □ + 3 = 7. Warm up those missing-number sentences and writing equations clicks.

opts.t: Both sides are the same amount

diagnostic.q: "A number plus 2 is 9." Which number sentence shows this?

learn.heading: What is an equation?
learn.intro: An equation is a number sentence with an equals sign. The = says the two sides are worth the same. A box □ or a letter stands for the unknown number.
examples: □ + 3 = 7 means "some number plus 3 equals 7." The box is 4 because 4 + 3 = 7.
Words become an equation: "5 more than a number is 8" → n + 5 = 8.

tiers.intro: Find the missing number one step at a time.

items.q: A. What goes in the box? □ + 2 = 5
B. What goes in the box? □ + 4 = 6
C. What goes in the box? 1 + □ = 5

tiers.intro: Match the words to the equation.

items.q: A. "A number plus 4 equals 9." Which equation?
items.hint: "Plus 4" means add 4 to the number n, and "equals 9" gives = 9.

items.q: B. In n + 4 = 9, what number is n?
A. "7 is the same as a number plus 2." Which equation?
items.hint: "Is the same as" is the = sign; the number plus 2 is n + 2.

items.q: B. In 7 = n + 2, what number is n?

exit.q: 1. "A number plus 6 is 10." Which equation shows this?
exit.hint: Add 6 to the number n, then set it equal to 10.
